Summary of Findings (Required): CYP2B6*2 (c.64C>T, p.R22C) - Normal function: CYP2B6*2 is assigned normal function based on in vivo (heterozygous patients) and in vitro studies. Multiple studies evaluating efavirenz plasma concentrations among patients with HIV found that CYP2B6*2 heterozygotes had similar efavirenz plasma concentrations compared to the *1 reference (21896912, 22471906, 23254426, 23399569). A study evaluating efavirenz content in hair samples among HIV patients taking efavirenz for more than three months, similarly found that CYP2B6*2 did not impact long term efavirenz exposure (26655325). Additionally, one in vitro study using human liver microsomes (17559344) and two in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems (12642465, 30201214) found that CYP2B6*2 had similar enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference. Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function based on strong evidence.

CYP2B6*3 (c.777C>A, p.S259R) - Uncertain function: CYP2B6*3 is assigned uncertain function. In vitro studies using cDNA expression systems demonstrated that CYP2B6*3 had similar enzyme activity or slightly decreased activity compared to the *1 reference (12642465, 30201214). This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

CYP2B6*4 (c.785A>G, p.K262R) - Increased function: CYB2B6*4 is assigned increased function based on in vivo (heterozygous patients only) and in vitro studies. In clinical studies, CYP2B6*4 heterozygotes had significantly increased metabolism of several substrates such as bupropion (14515060, 29637923), nicotine (17015050) and efavirenz (17235330). Numerically higher methadone clearance was observed in CYP2B6*4 carriers, albeit samples sizes were small (26389554, 35538637). Although two studies did not find increased efavirenz clearance compared to the *1 reference (23399569, 23254426), the experts did not find these results convincing due to the small sample size. Multiple in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems showed that CYP2B6*4 has increased activity compared to the *1 reference towards efavirenz, methadone, bupropion nicotine, artemether and selegiline, (12642465, 21746968, 21821736, 31324697, 32238417). One in vitro study using cDNA expression system showed no difference in activity using bupropion as a probe (21659470), but the experts did not find this evidence convincing. Some studies found CYP2B6*4 had less activity compared to the *1 reference towards ketamine, nicotine, cyclophosphamide and ifosphamide (21821736, 21976622, 25934575, 30085944, 30906561). This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. Therefore, consensus among experts was increased function based on moderate evidence, but effects may be substrate-dependent.

CYP2B6*5 (c.1459C>T, p.R487C) - Normal function: CYP2B6*5 is assigned normal function based on in vivo (heterozygous and homozygous patients) and in vitro studies. Multiple studies evaluating efavirenz plasma concentrations among patients with HIV found that carriers of the CYP2B6*5 allele had similar efavirenz plasma concentrations compared to the *1 reference (20662624, 20841522, 22354160, 23254426, 23399569, 23990572). A study evaluating efavirenz content in hair samples among HIV patients taking efavirenz for more than three months found that the CYP2B6*5 allele did not impact long term efavirenz exposure (26655325). In human liver microsomes, decreased protein expression and/or activity was reported (11470993, 17559344). In in vitro expression systems, activity ranged from decreased (32238417, 31324697) to no change (21659470) and increased (12642465, 25897175) activity compared to the *1 reference with various substrates. Since differences in protein expression and enzyme activity observed in vitro were not reflected by meaningful differences in vivo in numerous clinical studies (20662624, 20841522, 22354160, 23254426, 23399569, 23990572, 26389554), consensus among experts was normal function based on strong evidence.

CYP2B6*6 (c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect and c.785A>G, p.K262R) - Decreased function: CYP2B6*6 is assigned decreased function based on in vivo (homozygous and heterozygous patients) and in vitro studies. Significantly lower protein expression and activity was found in human liver microsomes, and increased plasma exposure of CYP2B6 substrates was found in healthy volunteers and patients. In a panel of 87 human liver microsome samples, the CYP2B6*6 allele was associated with significantly decreased protein expression and activity, measured by bupropion 4-hydroxylation and efavirenz 8-hydroxylation (17559344). The mechanism was predominantly aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T (but not c.785A>G) leading to reduced content of functional mRNA, protein, and activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). Multiple in vitro studies using human liver microsomes (17559344, 22232427, (23550066) and cDNA expression systems (21659470, 32238417, 35538637) found that CYP2B6*6 has decreased enzyme activity and metabolism of efavirenz, bupropion, methadone and ketamine. Although two other in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems found increased activity (12642465, 30201214), the experts did not find these results convincing. This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. Multiple studies evaluating efavirenz plasma concentrations among patients with HIV found that CYP2B6*6 carriers had higher efavirenz concentrations compared to the *1 reference (e.g., 15194512, 17235330, 27799204). A study evaluating efavirenz content in hair samples among HIV patients taking efavirenz for more than three months similarly found that CYP2B6*6 was associated with greater long term efavirenz exposure (26655325). Other studies using c.516G>T as a tagSNP for CYP2B6*6 (15622315) showed that G/T and T/T genotypes (compared to G/G) were associated with significantly higher efavirenz plasma exposure during HIV therapy. Several studies found that plasma exposure of the active bupropion metabolite hydroxybupropion was less in CYP2B6*6 carriers than in the *1 reference, although bupropion concentration and clearance were unchanged (23344581, 25565674, 29756345, 34752647). Multiple clinical studies of methadone, evaluating metabolism and/or clearance, have shown that methadone plasma exposure (S- more than R-) is greater and/or metabolism and clearance is less in CYP2B6*6 carriers compared to the *1 reference (16338275, 17178267, 17329992, 25456329, 26389554, 28723731, 32302325 ). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function based on strong evidence.

CYP2B6*7 (c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R and c.1459C>T, p.R487C) - Decreased function: CYB2B6*7 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*7 contains the variants which define CYP2B6*6 in addition to a third SNP (c.1459C>T). Since this is a rare variant, there are no data from human liver microsomes or clinical trials regarding its function. Data from several in vitro expression systems are inconclusive: some studies show decreased activity (31324697, 32238417, 3553863) while others exhibit increased activity (12642465, 21659470, 30201214). This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

CYP2B6*8 (c.415A>G, p.K139E) - No function: CYP2B6*8 is assigned no function. Multiple in vitro studies using human liver microsomes (14551287) and cDNA expression systems (15190123, 20517174, 21659470, 21746968, 30201214) found that CYP2B6*8 had no enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference with various substrates. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on limited evidence.

CYP2B6*9 (c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect) - Decreased function: CYP2B6*9 is assigned decreased function. When c.516G>T occurs alone, it defines CYP2B6*9. The c.516G>T variant causes aberrant splicing (14551287, 18171905, 24260284) and is in high linkage disequilibrium with c.785A>G, which is part of the CYP2B6*6 haplotype. c.516G>T is also present in several other haplotypes (e.g., CYP2B6*7, *13, *19, *20, *26, *34, *36, *37 and *38). These haplotypes make it difficult to ascertain the presence of *9 and its clinical function. Although several studies have reported a significant association between c.516G>T and efavirenz exposure (over 3-fold increase) in patient plasma and/or hair samples (15622315, 22927450, 23080225), these associations were erroneously attributed to CYP2B6*9 rather than *6. Several in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems show that CYP2B6*9 decreases activity towards efavirenz, bupropion, methadone, ketamine, and nicotine (21659470, 30085944, 30906561, 32238417, 35538637), while others have shown no or increased activity (11243870, 30201214). This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. At present, functional information is informed by the fact that alleles with c.516G>T (e.g., CYP2B6*6) have been associated with decreased function (see description for CYP2B6*6 allele). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function based on strong evidence.

CYP2B6*10 (c.62A>T, p.Q21L and c.64C>T, p.R22C) - Uncertain function: CYP2B6*10 is assigned uncertain function. In vitro studies using cDNA expression systems (21746968, 30201214) demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference. However, consensus among experts was uncertain function based on inadequate evidence as it could not be determined if diplotypes containing this allele would be phenotypically distinct from diplotypes comprising two normal function alleles.

CYP2B6*11 (c.136A>G, p.M46V) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*11 is assigned uncertain function. In vitro studies using cDNA expression systems demonstrated decreased or no enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference (20517174, 21746968, 15190123, 30201214). Some studies with heterozygous and homozygous CYP2B6*11 patients showed that this allele is associated with increased exposure of efavirenz (27045425, 26779253, 28886044), while other clinical studies (25303294, 23734829, 19916993, 23778320) did not consistently demonstrate decreased enzyme activity or altered PK with efavirenz. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function based on consistent in vitro data but limited and inconsistent clinical evidence.

CYP2B6*12 (c.296G>A, p.G99E) - No function: CYB2B6*12 is assigned no function. Multiple in vitro studies using human liver microsomes (15190123) and cDNA expression systems (20517174, 21746968, 30201214) found that CYP2B6*12 has no protein expression or enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference with various substrates. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on limited evidence.

CYP2B6*13 (c.415A>G, p.K139E; c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R) - No function: CYP2B6*13 is assigned no function. CYP2B6*13 contains the same variants as CYP2B6*6, which is assigned decreased function, and CYP2B6*8 which is assigned no function. Most in vitro studies including those using cDNA expression systems (15190123, 20517174, 30201214) and human liver microsomes (17559344) found that CYP2B6*13 has no enzyme activity; only one in vitro study using a cDNA expression system showed residual activity for artemether metabolism compared to the *1 reference. This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on limited evidence.

CYP2B6*14 (c.419G>A, p.R140Q) - Uncertain function: CYP2B6*14 is assigned uncertain function. In vitro studies using human liver microsomes (17559344, 15190123) and cDNA expression systems (15190123, 20517174, 30201214, 21746968) found that CYP2B6*14 has decreased enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference. However, due to the lack of any in vivo or clinical evidence, consensus among experts was uncertain clinical function based on limited evidence.

CYP2B6*15 (c.1172T>A, p.I391N) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*15 is assigned uncertain function. In vitro studies using human liver microsomes (17559344, 15190123) and cDNA expression systems (15190123, 20517174, 21746968, 30201214) showed decreased to no enzyme activity for CYP2B6*15 compared to the *1 reference (20662624). However, due to the lack of any in vivo or clinical evidence, consensus among experts was uncertain function based on limited evidence.

CYP2B6*17 (c.76A>T, p.T26S; c.83A>G, p.D28G; c.85C>A+c.86G>C, p.R29T) – Normal function: CYP2B6*17 is assigned normal function. A study evaluating efavirenz content in hair samples among HIV patients taking efavirenz for more than three months found that long term efavirenz exposure was not impacted by the CYP2B6*17 allele (26655325). Additionally, CYP2B6*17 had similar enzyme activity in vitro using cDNA expression systems compared to the *1 reference (16272958, 30201214, 32238417), but decreased activity for methadone (35538637). This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function based on limited evidence.

CYP2B6*18 (c. 983T>C, p.I328T) – No function: CYP2B6*18 is assigned no function (note that the formerly assigned CYP2B6*16 allele is now a suballele of CYP2B6*18). Several clinical studies indicate that CYP2B6*18 is strongly associated with higher plasma efavirenz exposure in patients with HIV (18281305, 23080225, 26655325). Studies evaluating efavirenz content in hair samples among patients with HIV taking efavirenz for more than three months found that patients with the CYP2B6*18 allele had nearly double efavirenz exposure compared to the *1 reference (26655325, 34115651). Another study evaluating plasma efavirenz concentrations in patients with HIV found three-fold higher efavirenz concentrations in patients carrying CYP2B6*18 compared to the *1 reference (16495778). Furthermore, CYP2B6*18 is associated with significantly higher exposure of nevirapine in plasma (18281305, 25878720) and hair (34115651) and lower exposure of hydroxybupropion after bupropion administration (23344581). Lower clearance of methadone compared to the *1 reference was found in a CYP2B6*18 carrier (35538637). Several in vitro studies found that CYP2B6*18 exhibited undetectable (or markedly decreased) protein expression and/or activity towards different probe substrates including efavirenz, bupropion, ketamine, and methadone, in several cDNA expression systems (16272958, 16495778, 20517174, 21746968, 25897175, 30085944, 30201214, 32238417, 35538637). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on strong evidence.

CYP2B6*19 (c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R; c.1006C>T, p.R336C) – Decreased function: CYP2B6*19 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*19 contains c.516G>T which causes aberrant splicing (see CYP2B6*6 for more information). CYP2B6*19 has a unique SNP, c.1006C>T, which discriminates it from other alleles with c.516G>T. Several in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems found decreased enzyme activity for CYP2B6*19 compared to the *1 reference with various substrates (16272958, 21746968, 30201214, 30085944, 31324697, 32238417, 35538637). As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

CYP2B6*20 (c.503C>T, p.T168I; c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R) – Decreased function: CYP2B6*20 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*20 contains c.516G>T which causes aberrant splicing (see CYP2B6*6 for more information). Three in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems found decreased enzyme activity of CYP2B6*20 compared to the *1 reference with various substrates (16272958, 21746968, 30201214). As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

CYP2B6*21 (c.1282C>A, p.P428T) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*21 is assigned uncertain function. In vitro studies using cDNA expression systems found no or severely decreased enzyme activity for CYP2B6*21 (20517174, 21746968, 16272958, 30201214). However, there was no in vivo data available or data in human liver microsomes. Therefore, the consensus among experts was uncertain function based on inadequate evidence.

CYP2B6*22 (c.-82T>C) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*22 is assigned uncertain function. One study in human liver microsomes found that CYP2B6*22 had increased enzyme activity towards efavirenz and bupropion compared to the *1 reference (17559344). A second study found increased levels of CYP2B6*22 mRNA expression levels in human liver tissue and enzyme activity in liver microsomes compared to the *1 reference (15722458). A recent study using human liver tissues did not find increased mRNA content or activity (35194103); this study was limited by having only two samples genotyped as CYP2B6*1*22. Therefore, the consensus among experts was uncertain function based on inadequate evidence.

CYP2B6*23 (c.1375A>G, p.M459V) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*23 is assigned uncertain function. One in vitro cDNA expression study found evidence of higher expression which did not cause a change in activity (30201214), while another study showed slightly increased activity (21746968). Therefore, the consensus among experts was uncertain function based on inadequate evidence.

CYP2B6*24 (c.1427G>A, p.G476D) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*24 is assigned uncertain function. In vitro studies using cDNA expression systems found that CYP2B6*24 had no enzyme activity towards several substrates compared to the *1 reference (20517174, 21746968, 30201214). However, there was no in vivo data available or data in human liver microsomes. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function based on inadequate evidence.

CYP2B6*25 (c.1454A>T, p.Q485L) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*25 is assigned uncertain function. In vitro studies using cDNA expression systems showed no significant changes in enzyme activity and/or protein expression compared to the *1 reference (20517174, 21746968, 30201214). However, there were no in vivo data available or data in human liver microsomes. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function based on inadequate evidence.

CYP2B6*26 (c.499C>G, p.P167A; c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R) – Decreased function: CYP2B6*26 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*26 contains c.516G>T which causes aberrant splicing (see CYP2B6*6 for more information). One study evaluating efavirenz plasma concentrations among patients with HIV found CYP2B6*26 was associated with elevated efavirenz concentrations compared to the *1 reference and has similar function to CYP2B6*6. This was demonstrated by similar efavirenz concentrations among CYP2B6 *6/*26 subjects CYP2B6 *6/*6 subjects (17918089). Although one in vitro study using a cDNA expression system found that CYP2B6*26 demonstrated increased enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference (30201214), the experts did not find these results convincing as it has previously been shown that in vitro expression systems may not adequately reflect CYP2B6*6 expression and function, and therefore also not be a suitable system to assess CYP2B6*26. As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

CYP2B6*27 (c.593T>C, p.M198T) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*27 is assigned uncertain function. Three in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems found that CYP2B6*27 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference for most substrates, although increased activity was seen for one non-drug probe substrate (21746968, 17235330, 30201214). This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. One subject with the CYP2B6*6/*27 genotype had an elevated efavirenz AUC compared to the *1 reference (17235330). Consensus was not achieved among experts due to insufficient evidence of this variant in human subjects. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned based on inadequate evidence.

CYP2B6*28 (c.917C>G, p.T306S; c.1132C>T, p.R378X) – No function: CYP2B6*28 is assigned no function. CYP2B6*28 is defined by a nonsense mutation which results in a premature stop codon resulting in a truncated protein. One study evaluating efavirenz plasma concentrations in patients with HIV found a subject with CYP2B6 *6/*28 genotype had elevated efavirenz AUC compared to the *1 reference and CYP2B6 *6/*6 subjects (17235330). In vitro studies using cDNA expression systems found that CYP2B6*28 demonstrated no enzyme activity (17235330, 20517174). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on limited evidence.

CYP2B6*29 (CYP2B7-CYP2B6 hybrid gene) – Decreased function: CYP2B6*29 is assigned decreased function. This allele is a hybrid consisting of CYP2B7 (exons 1-4) and CYP2B6 (exons 5-9) harboring 23 amino acid changes compared to the protein encoded by the CYP2B6*1 reference. Among those is c.516G>T which is one of the function-altering variants defining CYP2B6*6. CYP2B6*29 was identified in a subject with an elevated efavirenz AUC compared to the *1 reference and similar to those seen in patients carrying one decreased function allele (17885627). One in vitro study found that primary human hepatocytes of subjects with a CYP2B6*29 allele had slightly lower protein expression and enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference (28181240). The same study also showed that the AUC for 4-OH-cyclophosphamide was significantly lower for patients with a CYP2B6*1/*29 genotype compared to *1/*1 (28181240). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function based on limited evidence.

CYP2B6*30 (CYP2B7-CYP2B6 hybrid gene) – No function: CYP2B6*30 is assigned no function. This allele has a hybrid gene consisting of CYP2B6 (exons 1-4) and CYP2B7 (exons 5-9) harboring 15 amino acid changes compared to the protein encoded by the CYP2B6*1 reference. Among those is a premature stop codon (p.R378X) which is also found in CYP2B6*28 (23164804). This allele has been found in a duplication structure; it remains uncertain, however, whether the other gene in the duplication is a *1, *4 or *6 (or potentially other). Although CYP2B6*30 has no function, the function of this duplication allele depends on the nature of the other gene copy. One in vitro study found that primary human hepatocytes of subjects with a CYP2B6*30 had significantly lower mRNA and protein expression and enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference (28181240). The same study also showed that the AUC for 4-OH-cyclophosphamide was significantly lower for patients with *1/*30 compared to *1/*1 (28181240). In this study, the presence of a CYP2B6*30 was inferred by subjects having three gene copies and sequence analysis of the *30; however, the study did not provide any information regarding the nature of the second gene copy of the duplication. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on limited evidence.

CYP2B6*31 (c.937C>A, p.L313I) – Normal function: CYP2B6*31 is assigned normal function. A study evaluating efavirenz content in hair samples among patients with HIV taking efavirenz for more than three months found CYP2B6*31 did not impact long term efavirenz content (26655325). An in vitro study using a cDNA expression system similarly found CYP2B6*31 demonstrated similar enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference (30201214). Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function based on limited evidence.

CYP2B6*32 (c.1219G>A, p.A407T) – Normal function: CYP2B6*32 is assigned normal function. A study evaluating efavirenz content in hair samples among patients with HIV taking efavirenz for more than three months found CYP2B6*32 did not impact long term efavirenz content (26655325) compared to the *1 reference. Although one in vitro study using a cDNA expression system found that CYP2B6*32 demonstrated increased enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference (30201214), the experts did not find these results convincing. Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function based on limited evidence.

CYP2B6*33 (c.1459C>A, p.R487S) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*33 is assigned uncertain function. In vitro studies using cDNA expression systems found CYP2B6*33 demonstrated activity similar to the *1 reference or increased enzyme activity (23418033, 30201214). This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function based on inadequate evidence as it could not be determined if phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2B6*34 (c.-82T>C, c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R; c.1459C>A, p.R487S) – Decreased function: CYP2B6*34 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*34 contains c.516G>T which causes aberrant splicing (see CYP2B6*6 for more information) (23418033). Although one in vitro study using a cDNA expression system found that CYP2B6*34 demonstrated increased enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference (30201214), the experts did not find these results convincing as it has previously been shown that in vitro expression systems may not adequately reflect CYP2B6*6 expression and function, and therefore also not be a suitable system to assess CYP2B6*34. As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

CYP2B6*35 (c.-82T>C; c.329G>T, p.G110V; c.341T>C, p.I114T; c.444G>T, p.E148D; c.593T>C, p.M198T; c.835G>C, p.A279P) – Uncertain function: CYB2B6*35 is assigned uncertain function. Two in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems provided inconclusive evidence for CYP2B6*35 function (23418033, 30201214). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function based on inadequate evidence.

CYP2B6*36 (c.-82T>C, c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R) – Decreased function: CYB2B6*36 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*36 contains c.516G>T which causes aberrant splicing (see CYP2B6*6 for more information) (23418033). As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

CYP2B6*37 (c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.548T>G, p.V183G; c.785A>G, p.K262R) – Decreased function: CYB2B6*37 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*37 contains c.516G>T which causes aberrant splicing (see CYP2B6*6 for more information) (23418033). Multiple in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems found that CYP2B6*37 has no enzyme activity with bupropion and efavirenz compared to the *1 reference (23418033, 30201214). However, there was no in vivo data or data for liver microsomes. As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

CYP2B6*38 contains the variants which define CYP2B6*6 in addition to a third variant which causes a premature stop codon. CYP2B6*38 was identified in a patient taking efavirenz who developed neurologic symptoms and was found to have an efavirenz plasma concentration 40-fold above normal limit. Efavirenz was discontinued and efavirenz plasma concentrations returned to normal after 10 weeks in addition to resolution of neurologic symptoms (23640958). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on limited evidence.
